main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Temporally Augmented Patient Contrastive Learning of Representations (TA-PCLR)","Patient-based contrastive learning with temporal augmentations (zero-masking, random cropping)","Self-supervised pretraining on large-scale unlabeled ECG data"],"problems":["Improving feature extraction from ECGs for downstream tasks with limited labeled data","Enhancing generalization and performance of ECG foundation models","Addressing the scarcity of labeled medical data for deep learning"],"datasets":["A newly constructed multi-center dataset of over six million unlabeled ECGs from four cohorts across three continents","An open-source external ECG cohort for validation"],"metrics":["Performance on downstream supervised tasks (e.g., classification accuracy, AUROC, etc.)","Comparison to other pretraining approaches and fully supervised deep networks"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance on open-source external cohort (relative)","value":"Surpasses other pretraining approaches and outperforms an ensemble of fully supervised deep networks on some labels"},{"metric":"State-of-the-art results (relative)","value":"Outperforms other, much larger, self-supervised foundation models and is close to highly optimized fully supervised benchmarks"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Temporally Augmented Patient Contrastive Learning (TA-PCLR) that incorporates temporal augmentations into patient-based contrastive learning for ECGs","Construction and use of a new large-scale, multi-center ECG dataset (over six million ECGs from three continents) for pretraining","Demonstration that temporal augmentations (e.g., zero-masking, random cropping) improve the quality of learned representations for ECGs","Empirical evidence that the proposed foundation model outperforms existing self-supervised and some fully supervised models on external validation"]}

methods: Temporally Augmented Patient Contrastive Learning of Representations (TA-PCLR)
Patient-based contrastive learning with temporal augmentations (zero-masking, random cropping)
Self-supervised pretraining on large-scale unlabeled ECG data
problems: Improving feature extraction from ECGs for downstream tasks with limited labeled data
Enhancing generalization and performance of ECG foundation models
Addressing the scarcity of labeled medical data for deep learning
datasets: A newly constructed multi-center dataset of over six million unlabeled ECGs from four cohorts across three continents
An open-source external ECG cohort for validation
metrics: Performance on downstream supervised tasks (e.g., classification accuracy, AUROC, etc.)
Comparison to other pretraining approaches and fully supervised deep networks

results.value: Surpasses other pretraining approaches and outperforms an ensemble of fully supervised deep networks on some labels
Outperforms other, much larger, self-supervised foundation models and is close to highly optimized fully supervised benchmarks

novelty_claims: Introduction of Temporally Augmented Patient Contrastive Learning (TA-PCLR) that incorporates temporal augmentations into patient-based contrastive learning for ECGs
Construction and use of a new large-scale, multi-center ECG dataset (over six million ECGs from three continents) for pretraining
Demonstration that temporal augmentations (e.g., zero-masking, random cropping) improve the quality of learned representations for ECGs
Empirical evidence that the proposed foundation model outperforms existing self-supervised and some fully supervised models on external validation

methods: Development of ECGFounder, a general-purpose ECG foundation model trained on over 10 million ECGs with 150 label categories
Use of real-world ECG annotations from cardiology experts for model training
Novel pre-processing and training method to handle incomplete annotations in real-world data
Lead augmentation technique to train and maintain high performance on single-lead ECGs
Fine-tuning ECGFounder for various downstream tasks (demographic analysis, clinical event detection, cross-modality diagnosis)
problems: Insufficient database sample sizes for ECG AI models
Inadequate generalization of ECG models across multiple domains (regions, ethnic groups, diagnostic variations)
Performance gap between single-lead and multi-lead ECG analyses
Lack of large-scale, diverse, and publicly available ECG databases
Difficulty in developing general-purpose AI-ECG models that can be adapted to a wide range of downstream tasks
datasets: Harvard-Emory ECG Database (over 10 million ECGs, 150 label categories)
Internal validation sets (not specifically named)
External validation sets (not specifically named)
metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve)
Regression metrics (for age, LVEF, NTproBNP, etc.)

results.value: Strong generalization across various diagnoses (exact values not specified)
Outperforms baseline models in demographic analysis, clinical event detection, and cross-modality cardiac rhythm diagnosis

novelty_claims: ECGFounder is the largest and most comprehensive ECG foundation model to date, trained on over 10 million clinically annotated ECGs covering 150 diagnostic categories.
The model demonstrates expert-level performance and strong generalization across multiple domains, including single-lead and multi-lead ECGs.
Introduction of a novel pre-processing and training method to handle incomplete annotations in real-world ECG data.
Lead augmentation technique enables high diagnostic performance on single-lead ECGs, addressing a key limitation of previous models.
ECGFounder is versatile, supporting a wide range of downstream tasks and outperforming traditional models when fine-tuned.
The model and labeled data will be publicly released, promoting further research and development.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Patient Contrastive Learning of Representations (PCLR)","Supervised pre-training (baseline)","Unsupervised pre-training with an autoencoder (baseline)","Contrastive multi ECG-segment pre-training (baseline)"],"problems":["Scarcity of labeled training data for supervised machine learning in healthcare","Learning expressive and performant representations from large-scale unlabeled ECG data","Improving performance on clinical ECG tasks with limited labeled data"],"datasets":["Large healthcare system dataset with over 3.2 million 12-lead ECGs"],"metrics":["Performance increase (relative improvement)","Task-specific metrics (e.g., classification accuracy, regression error)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average performance increase (PCLR vs. training from scratch)","value":"51% across six training set sizes and four tasks"},{"metric":"Average performance benefit (PCLR vs. other pre-training models)","value":"47% across tasks"},{"metric":"Average performance benefit (PCLR vs. best model for each task)","value":"9%"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Patient Contrastive Learning of Representations (PCLR) for ECG modeling using contrastive learning on patient-level data.","Demonstration that PCLR representations are expressive, performant, and practical across multiple clinical tasks.","Significant performance improvements over both training from scratch and other pre-training approaches.","Release of PCLR implementation for public use."]}

methods: Patient Contrastive Learning of Representations (PCLR)
Supervised pre-training (baseline)
Unsupervised pre-training with an autoencoder (baseline)
Contrastive multi ECG-segment pre-training (baseline)
problems: Scarcity of labeled training data for supervised machine learning in healthcare

Improving performance on clinical ECG tasks with limited labeled data
datasets: Large healthcare system dataset with over 3.2 million 12-lead ECGs
metrics: Task-specific metrics (e.g., classification accuracy, regression error)

results.metric: Average performance increase (PCLR vs. training from scratch)

results.metric: Average performance benefit (PCLR vs. other pre-training models)
Average performance benefit (PCLR vs. best model for each task)

novelty_claims: Introduction of Patient Contrastive Learning of Representations (PCLR) for ECG modeling using contrastive learning on patient-level data.
Demonstration that PCLR representations are expressive, performant, and practical across multiple clinical tasks.
Significant performance improvements over both training from scratch and other pre-training approaches.
Release of PCLR implementation for public use.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["3KG: physiologically-inspired contrastive learning using 3D augmentations of 12-lead ECGs in VCG and ECG spaces","Spatial and temporal augmentations of ECG signals","Encoding leads independently and treating them as positive views for contrastive learning"],"problems":["Label-efficient learning for ECG interpretation with limited labeled data","Improving representation learning for 12-lead ECGs using self-supervised methods","Closing the performance gap between supervised and self-supervised learning for ECG analysis, especially at low label fractions"],"datasets":["PhysioNet 2020 challenge training data"],"metrics":["Mean AUC (Area Under the Curve)","AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve)"],"results":[{"metric":"Mean AUC improvement over best self-supervised baseline (1% labeled data)","value":"9.1%"},{"metric":"AUROC (3KG, 1% labeled data)","value":"0.826"},{"metric":"AUROC (best previous self-supervised, 1% labeled data)","value":"0.757"},{"metric":"AUROC (fully supervised, 100% labeled data)","value":"0.898"}],"novelty_claims":["First physiologically-inspired contrastive learning approach for 12-lead ECGs using 3D augmentations in VCG space","Demonstrates that combining spatial and temporal augmentations produces the strongest representations for ECGs","Shows that 3KG closes the gap to fully supervised performance at low label fractions, outperforming previous self-supervised ECG methods","Investigates the effect of physiologically-inspired pretraining on different disease subgroups, showing greatest gains for conduction and rhythm abnormalities","Provides a flexible framework for incorporating other self-supervised strategies and modality-specific augmentations for biomedical signals"]}

methods: 3KG: physiologically-inspired contrastive learning using 3D augmentations of 12-lead ECGs in VCG and ECG spaces
Spatial and temporal augmentations of ECG signals
Encoding leads independently and treating them as positive views for contrastive learning
problems: Label-efficient learning for ECG interpretation with limited labeled data
Improving representation learning for 12-lead ECGs using self-supervised methods
Closing the performance gap between supervised and self-supervised learning for ECG analysis, especially at low label fractions
datasets: PhysioNet 2020 challenge training data
metrics: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve)

results.metric: Mean AUC improvement over best self-supervised baseline (1% labeled data)

novelty_claims: First physiologically-inspired contrastive learning approach for 12-lead ECGs using 3D augmentations in VCG space
Demonstrates that combining spatial and temporal augmentations produces the strongest representations for ECGs
Shows that 3KG closes the gap to fully supervised performance at low label fractions, outperforming previous self-supervised ECG methods
Investigates the effect of physiologically-inspired pretraining on different disease subgroups, showing greatest gains for conduction and rhythm abnormalities
Provides a flexible framework for incorporating other self-supervised strategies and modality-specific augmentations for biomedical signals

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["ST-MEM (Spatio-Temporal Masked Electrocardiogram Modeling): a generative self-supervised learning framework based on Masked Autoencoder (MAE) architecture, designed for ECG data","Spatio-temporal patchifying of ECG signals to capture both spatial (across leads) and temporal (across time) relationships","Lead-wise shared decoder, learnable lead embeddings, and separation embedding for modeling ECG leads"],"problems":["Limited labeled ECG data for disease screening and arrhythmia classification","Existing self-supervised learning (SSL) methods for ECG do not effectively capture the spatio-temporal relationships inherent in multi-lead ECG signals","Need for generalizable ECG representations that work across different lead configurations (full 12-lead, reduced lead, single lead)"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Arrhythmia classification accuracy (in fine-tuning and linear evaluation settings)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes ST-MEM, a simple but effective ECG-specific generative self-supervised learning framework that leverages spatio-temporal masking and patchifying to capture both spatial and temporal relationships in ECG data","Demonstrates that ST-MEM can be structurally fine-tuned for reduced lead ECG, maintaining strong performance even with fewer leads","Shows through quantitative and qualitative analysis that the learned representations capture spatio-temporal relationships in ECGs","ST-MEM outperforms other SSL baseline methods in various experimental settings for arrhythmia classification tasks, including scenarios with scarce labeled data and reduced lead settings"]}

methods: ST-MEM (Spatio-Temporal Masked Electrocardiogram Modeling): a generative self-supervised learning framework based on Masked Autoencoder (MAE) architecture, designed for ECG data
Spatio-temporal patchifying of ECG signals to capture both spatial (across leads) and temporal (across time) relationships
Lead-wise shared decoder, learnable lead embeddings, and separation embedding for modeling ECG leads
problems: Limited labeled ECG data for disease screening and arrhythmia classification
Existing self-supervised learning (SSL) methods for ECG do not effectively capture the spatio-temporal relationships inherent in multi-lead ECG signals
Need for generalizable ECG representations that work across different lead configurations (full 12-lead, reduced lead, single lead)
metrics: Arrhythmia classification accuracy (in fine-tuning and linear evaluation settings)
novelty_claims: Proposes ST-MEM, a simple but effective ECG-specific generative self-supervised learning framework that leverages spatio-temporal masking and patchifying to capture both spatial and temporal relationships in ECG data
Demonstrates that ST-MEM can be structurally fine-tuned for reduced lead ECG, maintaining strong performance even with fewer leads
Shows through quantitative and qualitative analysis that the learned representations capture spatio-temporal relationships in ECGs
ST-MEM outperforms other SSL baseline methods in various experimental settings for arrhythmia classification tasks, including scenarios with scarce labeled data and reduced lead settings

First benchmark of multimodal ECG representation learning across six public datasets, covering over 100 cardiac conditions and multiple evaluation scenarios.
